flashcards.front: What is a list in R?
flashcards.back: A list is a data structure in R that can hold objects of different types (vectors, matrices, data frames, functions, or other lists) in an ordered collection.

flashcards.front: How do you create a list in R?
flashcards.back: Use the list() function: my_list <- list(element1, element2, ...). Each element can be of different data type.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between [] and [[]] when accessing list elements?
flashcards.back: [] returns a sub-list (still a list), while [[]] returns the actual element (the object itself, not a list). Use $ or [[n]] for direct access.

flashcards.front: How do you name components of a list?
flashcards.back: Use names(my_list) <- c("name1", "name2", ...) when creating, or use list(name1 = element1, name2 = element2) syntax.

flashcards.front: How do you add a new element to an existing list?
flashcards.back: Assign to a new index: my_list[[length(my_list) + 1]] <- new_element, or use my_list$new_name <- element.
unlist() converts a list into a vector by flattening all elements. It attempts to coerce all elements into a single atomic vector.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between lapply() and sapply()?
flashcards.back: lapply() applies a function to each element and always returns a list. sapply() tries to simplify the result to a vector or matrix if possible.

flashcards.front: How do you remove an element from a list?
flashcards.back: Set the element to NULL: my_list[[n]] <- NULL or my_list$name <- NULL. This removes the element and shifts subsequent indices.

flashcards.front: What is a nested list in R?
flashcards.back: A nested list is a list that contains other lists as its elements. Access nested elements using multiple [[]] operators: my_list[[1]][[2]].

flashcards.front: How do you check if an object is a list?
flashcards.back: Use is.list(my_object) which returns TRUE if the object is a list, FALSE otherwise.

flashcards.front: What is the str() function used for with lists?
flashcards.back: str() displays the structure of a list in a compact form, showing the data types and first few elements of each component.

flashcards.front: How do you combine two or more lists?
flashcards.back: Use the c() function: combined_list <- c(list1, list2, ...). This merges all lists into one.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a list and a vector in R?
flashcards.back: Vectors contain elements of the SAME type, while lists can contain elements of DIFFERENT types. Vectors are atomic, lists are recursive.

flashcards.front: How do you modify an existing element in a list?
flashcards.back: Simply reassign using the index or name: my_list[[1]] <- new_value or my_list$name <- new_value.

flashcards.front: Why are data frames considered special lists in R?
flashcards.back: Data frames are lists where each column is an element (all columns must have equal length). They combine list functionality with tabular structure.
